title: Customer Identification — Individual Natural Persons
description: Collect and verify the identity of individual natural person customers before establishing a business relationship or processing any transaction. Obtain the customer's full legal name as it appears on government-issued identification, date of birth, residential address (not a PO Box), nationality, and a government-issued identification number (SSN for US persons, national ID number, or passport number for non-US persons). Verify the identity through documentary methods by obtaining a clear copy of a valid, unexpired government-issued photo ID such as a passport, national identity card, or driver's licence. Cross-reference the provided information against the identity document. For remote onboarding, implement liveness detection and document authenticity verification using certified identity verification technology. Record the date and method of verification. If discrepancies are identified between provided information and documentary evidence, escalate to the compliance team before proceeding with onboarding.
threshold_triggers: Occasional transactions above applicable threshold (US: $3,000, EU: €15,000, SG: SGD 5,000)
documents_required: Valid government-issued photo ID (passport, national ID, driver's licence)
Proof of residential address (utility bill, bank statement within 3 months)
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1010.220 (CIP); EU: MiCA Art. 68 + AMLD5 Art. 13; UK: MLR 2017 Reg. 28; SG: MAS Notice PSN02 Para. 6; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook Ch. 2
risk_indicators: ID document appears altered or tampered
Customer provides inconsistent personal information
Customer is reluctant to provide identification
Name or date of birth does not match documentation
Customer uses multiple identities across platforms
ID is from a high-risk jurisdiction with known document fraud

title: Customer Identification — Legal Entity Customers
description: Collect and verify the identity of legal entity customers including corporations, partnerships, trusts, foundations, and other organisational structures. Obtain the entity's full legal name, registered address, jurisdiction of incorporation or registration, date of incorporation, registration number, Legal Entity Identifier (LEI) where available, and the nature and purpose of the business. Verify the entity's existence and legal status through official registry records such as the Companies House register (UK), SEC EDGAR (US), ACRA (Singapore), or equivalent national registries. Obtain and review the entity's constitutional documents including articles of incorporation, memorandum of association, partnership agreement, or trust deed as applicable. Verify the identity of authorised signatories and persons authorised to act on behalf of the entity. For complex multi-layered corporate structures, obtain an organisational chart showing the ownership chain up to the ultimate beneficial owners. Document the source of funds and source of wealth for the entity.
threshold_triggers: Change in corporate structure of existing entity customer
documents_required: Articles of incorporation / memorandum of association
systems: Bureau van Dijk (Orbis — corporate registry data)
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1010.230 (CDD Rule); EU: AMLD5 Art. 13(1)(b); UK: MLR 2017 Reg. 28(3); SG: MAS Notice PSN02 Para. 6; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook Ch. 2
risk_indicators: Entity registered in secrecy jurisdiction or tax haven
Complex multi-layered ownership structure with no apparent business rationale
Entity recently incorporated with no operating history
Nominee directors or shareholders used
Bearer shares in the ownership chain
Entity cannot provide audited financial statements
Registered address is a virtual office or mail forwarding service

title: Ultimate Beneficial Owner (UBO) Identification and Verification
description: Identify and verify the identity of all ultimate beneficial owners of legal entity customers. A UBO is defined as any natural person who ultimately owns or controls the customer entity. Under most jurisdictions, the ownership threshold is 25% or more of the shares, voting rights, or ownership interest in the entity, though some jurisdictions apply lower thresholds (10% for certain high-risk sectors). Where no natural person meets the ownership threshold, identify the natural person(s) who exercise control through other means such as board control, veto rights, or influence over key decisions. As a fallback, where no UBO can be identified through ownership or control, identify the senior managing official(s) of the entity. Trace the ownership chain through all intermediate holding companies, trusts, and other structures to identify the natural person(s) at the top of the chain. Verify the identity of each identified UBO using the same documentary and non-documentary methods as for individual customers. Record the nature and extent of each UBO's ownership interest or control. Update UBO information at least annually and when triggered by material changes.
threshold_triggers: All legal entity customers at onboarding
documents_required: UBO declaration form signed by authorised representative
Photo ID and proof of address for each identified UBO
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1010.230 (25% threshold); EU: AMLD5 Art. 3(6) (25% threshold); UK: MLR 2017 Reg. 5 (25% threshold); SG: MAS Notice PSN02 Para. 6; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook (25% threshold)
risk_indicators: Reluctance to disclose UBO information
UBO is a PEP or close associate of a PEP
UBO is resident in a sanctioned or high-risk jurisdiction
Nominee or straw-man arrangements obscuring true ownership
Frequent changes in UBO structure
UBO has adverse media indicating criminal activity

title: Sanctions Screening — Initial Onboarding
description: Screen all customers, beneficial owners, directors, and authorised signatories against applicable sanctions lists before establishing a business relationship. At minimum, screen against the OFAC Specially Designated Nationals and Blocked Persons List (SDN List), OFAC Consolidated Sanctions List, UN Security Council Consolidated List, EU Consolidated List of Persons Subject to Financial Sanctions, UK HM Treasury Consolidated List (Office of Financial Sanctions Implementation), MAS Designated Individuals and Entities List (Singapore), and UAE Local Terrorist List. For crypto-specific screening, also check known sanctioned wallet addresses against blockchain analytics databases. Screening must cover exact name matches and fuzzy matching to capture variations, transliterations, aliases, and common misspellings. Configure matching algorithms with appropriate sensitivity thresholds — typically 80-85% match score for initial screening with human review of all hits above threshold. Document all screening results, including negative (clear) results. Where a potential match is identified, escalate to the MLRO/compliance officer for determination before proceeding with the business relationship. True positive matches must result in immediate blocking of the relationship and filing of a blocking report with the relevant authority.
threshold_triggers: All beneficial owners and authorised signatories
documents_required: Customer identification information for screening input
Screening results report with match scores
systems: OFAC SDN search tool (free, basic screening)
regulatory_basis: US: OFAC regulations (31 CFR Part 501); EU: Regulation (EC) No 2580/2001; UK: Sanctions and Anti-Money Laundering Act 2018; SG: Terrorism (Suppression of Financing) Act; AE: Federal Decree-Law No. 20 of 2018
risk_indicators: Name match on any sanctions list
Customer connected to a sanctioned jurisdiction (Iran, North Korea, Syria, Cuba, Crimea, etc.)
Wallet address associated with sanctioned entity
Customer's source of funds traces to sanctioned country
Customer is on adverse media lists for sanctions evasion

title: Politically Exposed Person (PEP) Screening
description: Screen all customers, beneficial owners, and their close associates and family members against PEP databases at onboarding and on an ongoing basis. A PEP is defined as a natural person who is or has been entrusted with a prominent public function, including heads of state, senior government officials, senior judicial officials, senior military officials, senior executives of state-owned enterprises, and senior political party officials. Family members of PEPs (spouse, children, parents, siblings) and close associates (persons known to have joint beneficial ownership or close business relationships with a PEP) must also be identified. PEP status does not automatically preclude a business relationship but triggers mandatory enhanced due diligence. When a PEP is identified, obtain senior management approval before establishing or continuing the business relationship. Conduct enhanced source of funds and source of wealth verification. Implement enhanced ongoing monitoring with lower transaction thresholds and more frequent reviews. Document the rationale for accepting or continuing the PEP relationship. PEP status persists for at least 12-18 months after the person leaves their public function (exact period varies by jurisdiction).
threshold_triggers: Annual refresh of existing customer base
regulatory_basis: US: BSA/AML requirements for PEPs; EU: AMLD5 Art. 20-23; UK: MLR 2017 Reg. 35; SG: MAS Notice PSN02 Para. 10; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook Ch. 3
risk_indicators: Customer holds or has held a prominent public function
Family member or close associate of a PEP
Source of wealth inconsistent with known income from public function
Transaction patterns inconsistent with declared occupation
Customer from jurisdiction with high corruption index
Reluctance to provide source of wealth documentation

title: Customer Risk Assessment and Risk Scoring
description: Assign a risk rating to each customer based on a comprehensive assessment of customer risk factors, geographic risk factors, product/service risk factors, and delivery channel risk factors. Customer risk factors include the customer type (individual vs entity), occupation or business activity, PEP status, adverse media, and prior regulatory history. Geographic risk factors include the customer's country of residence, nationality, country of incorporation (for entities), and jurisdictions involved in transactions. Product/service risk factors include the types of crypto-assets traded, use of privacy coins, use of mixing or tumbling services, and involvement in high-risk DeFi protocols. Delivery channel risk factors include remote vs face-to-face onboarding, use of intermediaries, and complexity of the transaction chain. Apply a risk scoring methodology that weights each factor based on its relative importance and produces a composite risk score categorised as low, medium, high, or prohibited. Document the risk scoring methodology and ensure it is approved by senior management. High-risk customers trigger enhanced due diligence requirements. The risk assessment must be reviewed and updated at least annually or when triggered by material changes or suspicious activity indicators.
documents_required: Supporting documentation for each risk factor
Senior management approval for high-risk customers
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1010.210 (risk-based AML program); EU: AMLD5 Art. 8 (risk assessment); UK: MLR 2017 Reg. 18; SG: MAS Notice PSN02 Para. 5; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook Ch. 2
risk_indicators: Customer refuses to provide information needed for risk assessment
Multiple high-risk factors present simultaneously
Customer's stated purpose inconsistent with transaction patterns
Customer requests unusual or complex product arrangements
Prior SAR/STR filed against customer at another institution

title: Source of Funds Verification
description: Verify the source of funds for all customers, with the depth of verification proportionate to the customer's risk rating. Source of funds refers to the origin of the specific funds being used in the business relationship or transaction — where the money is coming from. For standard-risk customers, obtain a declaration of source of funds and verify against the customer's known profile (employment income, business revenue, investment returns, property sale, inheritance, etc.). For higher-risk customers and transactions above applicable thresholds, obtain documentary evidence supporting the declared source of funds such as bank statements showing the source of incoming transfers, employment contracts and payslips, business financial statements, property sale contracts, inheritance documentation, or investment portfolio statements. For crypto-specific source of funds, conduct blockchain analysis to trace the origin of crypto-assets being deposited, identifying the source wallets and any exposure to high-risk services (mixers, darknet markets, sanctioned addresses). Document the source of funds assessment and retain evidence for the required retention period. Escalate cases where the source of funds cannot be satisfactorily established or appears inconsistent with the customer's profile.
threshold_triggers: Enhanced requirement for high-risk customers and PEPs
documents_required: Blockchain analysis report for crypto deposits
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1010.210; EU: AMLD5 Art. 13(1)(c); UK: MLR 2017 Reg. 28(6); SG: MAS Notice PSN02 Para. 6; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook Ch. 2
risk_indicators: Source of funds inconsistent with customer's profile or occupation
Funds originate from high-risk jurisdiction
Crypto deposits from wallets associated with darknet markets or mixing services
Customer unable to explain source of large deposits
Structured deposits designed to avoid reporting thresholds
Funds routed through multiple intermediaries without apparent business purpose

title: Adverse Media Screening
description: Conduct adverse media screening on all customers, beneficial owners, and key individuals associated with entity customers at onboarding and on an ongoing basis. Adverse media screening involves searching global news sources, regulatory databases, law enforcement databases, and public records for negative information about the customer that may indicate involvement in financial crime, fraud, money laundering, terrorist financing, sanctions evasion, corruption, tax evasion, or other criminal activity. The screening should cover multiple languages and jurisdictions, and should include both current and historical adverse media. Use a combination of automated screening tools that continuously monitor media sources and manual searches for more nuanced investigations. Categorise adverse media hits by severity (confirmed criminal conviction, ongoing investigation, allegation, association) and by relevance to ML/TF risk. Document all adverse media findings and the determination made — whether the finding is relevant, and if so, what additional measures are required. Adverse media hits on existing customers should trigger a review of the customer's risk rating and may necessitate enhanced due diligence or SAR filing. Retain adverse media screening records for the required retention period.
threshold_triggers: Periodic refresh (at least annually for high-risk, every 2-3 years for standard)
Transaction monitoring alert triggers ad-hoc screening
documents_required: Analyst determination for each relevant hit
Updated risk assessment if material findings
regulatory_basis: US: BSA/AML program requirements; EU: AMLD5 Art. 13 (risk-based CDD); UK: MLR 2017 Reg. 28; SG: MAS Notice PSN02; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook
risk_indicators: Customer named in criminal proceedings
Customer associated with known financial crime schemes
Customer's business partners involved in sanctions evasion
Customer subject to regulatory enforcement action
Customer mentioned in leaked financial crime databases (e.g. Panama Papers, FinCEN Files)

title: Wallet Address Risk Screening — Crypto-Specific
description: Conduct risk screening on all cryptocurrency wallet addresses provided by the customer or associated with the customer's account. Use blockchain analytics tools to assess each wallet address for exposure to high-risk categories including sanctioned addresses (OFAC SDN, EU, UN designated addresses), darknet marketplace transactions, ransomware-associated addresses, terrorist financing-linked addresses, stolen funds and hack proceeds, mixer and tumbling service usage, fraud and scam addresses, and child exploitation material-related addresses. Assess both direct exposure (the wallet has transacted directly with a flagged address) and indirect exposure (the wallet's funds can be traced through intermediate hops to a flagged address). Configure risk thresholds appropriate to the firm's risk appetite — typically, any direct exposure to sanctioned, ransomware, terrorist financing, or CSAM-related addresses should trigger automatic blocking. Indirect exposure to mixers or darknet markets may be assessed on a proportional basis. Screen wallet addresses at onboarding, when a new withdrawal address is added, and on an ongoing basis as blockchain analytics databases are updated with new flagged addresses.
threshold_triggers: All new wallet addresses provided by customer
documents_required: Risk exposure summary for each wallet
regulatory_basis: US: OFAC compliance obligations; EU: MiCA Art. 79 + TFR; UK: MLR 2017 + OFSI compliance; SG: MAS Notice PSN02; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook Ch. 5
risk_indicators: Direct interaction with OFAC-listed addresses
Funds traced to darknet marketplaces
Significant mixer/tumbler usage (>10% of transaction volume)
Interaction with known ransomware addresses
Funds from exploited DeFi protocols or hacks
High velocity of transactions suggesting layering
Interaction with unregistered/unlicensed exchanges in high-risk jurisdictions

title: Customer Acceptance Decision and Documentation
description: Make a formal customer acceptance decision based on the totality of information collected during the onboarding process, including identity verification, UBO identification, sanctions screening, PEP screening, adverse media screening, source of funds assessment, wallet risk screening, and customer risk rating. The acceptance decision must be made by a person with appropriate authority — standard-risk customers may be approved by the onboarding team, medium-risk customers should require compliance officer review, and high-risk customers must receive senior management or MLRO approval. Document the acceptance decision including the rationale, any conditions attached to the relationship (enhanced monitoring, transaction limits, periodic review frequency), and the identity of the approver. Where a customer is rejected, document the reasons for rejection and retain the file. Ensure the rejection does not trigger a tipping-off violation — the customer should be given a neutral reason for the rejection without disclosing that it was triggered by AML/CFT concerns. Update the customer record management system with the onboarding status, risk rating, and review schedule.
documents_required: Completed KYC file with all supporting documents
Screening results (sanctions, PEP, adverse media, wallet)
Acceptance decision form with approver signature
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1010.210; EU: AMLD5 Art. 14; UK: MLR 2017 Reg. 27; SG: MAS Notice PSN02; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook
risk_indicators: Multiple risk factors identified during onboarding
Incomplete documentation despite repeated requests
Customer attempts to rush the onboarding process
Customer provides contradictory information
Customer has been rejected by other regulated firms

title: Transaction Monitoring — Rule-Based Detection
description: Implement and operate a transaction monitoring system that applies rule-based detection scenarios to identify potentially suspicious transactions in real time or near-real time. Monitoring rules must cover the following scenarios at minimum: transactions exceeding applicable reporting thresholds (US: $10,000 CTR, $3,000 Travel Rule; EU: €1,000 TFR; UK: £1,000 Travel Rule; SG: SGD 1,500 Travel Rule), structuring or smurfing (multiple transactions just below reporting thresholds), rapid movement of funds (deposit-trade-withdrawal within short timeframes suggesting pass-through), large value transactions inconsistent with customer profile or risk rating, transactions involving high-risk jurisdictions, transactions with sanctioned or flagged wallet addresses, unusual patterns in trading activity (wash trading indicators), and dormant accounts suddenly becoming active. Configure thresholds and parameters based on the firm's customer base, product mix, and risk profile. Generate alerts for transactions meeting rule criteria and route alerts to trained compliance analysts for investigation. Document the monitoring rule set, including the rationale for each rule and threshold. Review and update rules at least quarterly to address emerging typologies and regulatory changes.
threshold_triggers: Batch processing for lower-risk transaction types
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1022.320 (SAR requirement implies monitoring); EU: AMLD5 Art. 13(1)(d); UK: MLR 2017 Reg. 28(11); SG: MAS Notice PSN02 Para. 11; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook Ch. 4
risk_indicators: Transactions just below reporting thresholds (structuring)
Rapid deposit-withdrawal cycles
Transactions inconsistent with customer profile
High volume of P2P transfers to unhosted wallets
Interaction with newly created wallets with no transaction history
Use of privacy coins or mixing services

title: Transaction Monitoring — Blockchain Analytics
description: Supplement rule-based monitoring with continuous blockchain analytics monitoring of all on-chain transactions associated with customer wallets. Blockchain analytics provides visibility into the on-chain transaction history that traditional transaction monitoring cannot capture. Monitor customer deposit addresses for incoming transactions from high-risk sources including sanctioned addresses, darknet marketplaces, ransomware-associated wallets, stolen fund traces, mixer and tumbler services, and unregistered exchange hot wallets. Monitor customer withdrawal addresses for transactions to high-risk destinations. Track the provenance of funds through multiple hops to identify indirect exposure to illicit sources. Monitor for patterns of chain-hopping (moving funds across different blockchains to obfuscate the trail), use of DeFi protocols for layering, and interaction with newly deployed smart contracts that may be associated with scams or exploits. Generate alerts when on-chain activity exceeds configured risk thresholds and route to compliance analysts for investigation alongside traditional transaction monitoring alerts. Integrate blockchain analytics alerts with the case management system to provide analysts with a unified view of customer activity across both on-chain and off-chain dimensions.
threshold_triggers: All on-chain transactions to and from customer wallets
New wallet addresses added to customer accounts
Updates to blockchain analytics risk databases
regulatory_basis: US: OFAC sanctions compliance + BSA monitoring obligations; EU: MiCA + TFR; UK: MLR 2017 + OFSI obligations; SG: MAS Notice PSN02; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook
risk_indicators: Customer receives funds from OFAC-listed address
Funds traced to darknet marketplace (2+ hops)
Customer uses cross-chain bridges to obfuscate fund trail
Customer interacts with known exploit or rug-pull contracts
Significant portion of customer deposits from mixer services
Customer sends funds to newly created contract with no audit history

title: Periodic KYC Refresh and Re-verification
description: Conduct periodic reviews and refreshes of customer KYC information to ensure that the firm's records remain current and that the customer's risk profile accurately reflects their current circumstances. The frequency of KYC refresh is determined by the customer's risk rating: high-risk customers must be reviewed at least annually, medium-risk customers every two years, and low-risk customers every three years. The refresh process includes re-verifying the customer's identity information against current documentation, re-screening against sanctions lists, PEP databases, and adverse media sources, updating beneficial ownership information for entity customers, reviewing the customer's transaction patterns against their stated purpose of account and expected activity, reassessing the customer's risk rating based on updated information and transaction history, and verifying that the customer's source of funds and source of wealth remain consistent with their profile. Where material changes are identified, escalate for compliance review and update the risk rating accordingly. Document all refresh activities and their outcomes. Failure to complete a required KYC refresh within the prescribed timeframe should trigger restrictions on the customer's account until the refresh is completed.
threshold_triggers: Risk-based periodic schedule (annually for high-risk, 2 years for medium, 3 years for low)
Adverse media or screening hit on existing customer
Triggered by suspicious transaction monitoring alert
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1010.230 (ongoing CDD); EU: AMLD5 Art. 13(1)(d); UK: MLR 2017 Reg. 28(11); SG: MAS Notice PSN02 Para. 11; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook Ch. 2
risk_indicators: Customer's transaction volume significantly exceeds original expected activity
Customer's profile has changed materially (new business activities, new jurisdictions)
Customer fails to respond to refresh requests
New adverse media emerges about customer
Customer's UBO structure has changed without notification

title: Sanctions List Update Monitoring and Rescreening
description: Implement a process for monitoring updates to all applicable sanctions lists and rescreening the entire customer base against updated lists on a timely basis. Sanctions lists are updated frequently — OFAC updates its SDN list multiple times per week, the EU Consolidated List is updated regularly, and the UK Consolidated List is updated by OFSI as designations change. The firm must have automated processes in place to download and ingest updated sanctions list data as soon as it is published and to rescreen all active customers, beneficial owners, directors, and authorised signatories against the updated data. Screening must be completed within 24 hours of a list update for high-risk updates (new designations of crypto-specific entities or addresses) and within 48 hours for routine updates. Where a new match is identified on rescreening, immediately freeze the affected account and escalate to the MLRO/compliance officer. File a blocking report with the relevant sanctions authority within the required timeframe (OFAC: 10 business days; OFSI: as soon as practicable). Do not inform the customer of the reason for the freeze if doing so would constitute tipping off or sanctions evasion facilitation.
threshold_triggers: New OFAC, EU, UN, UK, or local sanctions designations
systems: Dow Jones Risk & Compliance (automated list updates)
regulatory_basis: US: OFAC regulations (strict liability); EU: Council Regulations on restrictive measures; UK: SAMLA 2018 + OFSI guidance; SG: Terrorism (Suppression of Financing) Act; AE: Federal AML/CFT Law
risk_indicators: Customer name matches newly designated entity/individual
Customer wallet address matches newly designated crypto address
Customer's counterparties include newly designated entities
Customer attempts to transact immediately after a relevant sanctions update

title: Currency Transaction Report (CTR) Filing
description: File Currency Transaction Reports with FinCEN for each transaction in currency (physical cash) exceeding $10,000 conducted by or on behalf of any person during a single business day. Multiple cash transactions must be aggregated if conducted by or on behalf of the same person and totalling more than $10,000 in a single day. CTRs must be filed within 15 calendar days of the transaction using FinCEN Form 112 through the BSA E-Filing system. For crypto businesses, CTR obligations primarily arise in connection with Bitcoin ATM operations, over-the-counter cash-for-crypto transactions, and other scenarios involving physical currency. CTRs are not required for crypto-to-crypto transactions or electronic funds transfers that do not involve physical currency. When preparing a CTR, record the identity of the person conducting the transaction (verified through government-issued photo ID), the identity of the person on whose behalf the transaction is being conducted (if different), the amount and type of transaction, and the date and time. Implement monitoring systems to detect structuring — the intentional breaking up of transactions to avoid the $10,000 CTR threshold, which is itself a federal crime under 31 U.S.C. § 5324.
threshold_triggers: Cash transactions exceeding $10,000 in a single business day
Aggregated cash transactions by same person exceeding $10,000 in a day
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1010.311 (CTR requirement); 31 U.S.C. § 5324 (anti-structuring)
risk_indicators: Cash transactions consistently at or just below $10,000
Multiple cash transactions by same person in short timeframes
Customer requests to break up a single large transaction into smaller ones
Customer appears nervous or evasive during cash transaction
Multiple persons conducting cash transactions for the same beneficiary

title: Suspicious Activity Report (SAR/STR) Filing Process
description: File Suspicious Activity Reports (SARs) or Suspicious Transaction Reports (STRs) with the appropriate Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU) when the firm knows, suspects, or has reasonable grounds to suspect that a transaction involves proceeds of criminal activity, is designed to evade reporting requirements, has no business or apparent lawful purpose, or involves the use of the firm's services to facilitate criminal activity. In the US, SARs must be filed with FinCEN within 30 calendar days of initial detection for transactions of $2,000 or more (MSB threshold). In the UK, SARs must be filed with the NCA as soon as practicable under POCA Section 330. In Singapore, STRs must be filed with the STRO without delay. In the EU, reports must be filed with the national FIU in accordance with AMLD requirements. When suspicious activity is identified, the compliance analyst must document the suspicious indicators, conduct additional investigation to gather relevant facts, prepare a narrative describing the suspicious activity including the who, what, when, where, why, and how, and submit the SAR/STR for MLRO review and approval before filing. SAR filing is strictly confidential — the firm must not disclose to the customer or any other person that a SAR has been filed (tipping off prohibition). Retain a copy of the SAR and all supporting documentation for at least five years.
threshold_triggers: UK: Any suspicious activity (no monetary threshold under POCA)
EU: Any suspicious activity per national FIU requirements
SG: Any suspicious activity per MAS Notice PSN02
AE: Any suspicious activity per VARA AML/CFT Rulebook
documents_required: SAR form per national FIU requirements — EU/UK/SG/AE
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1022.320; UK: POCA 2002 Section 330; EU: AMLD5 Art. 33; SG: MAS Notice PSN02 Para. 12; AE: Federal Decree-Law No. 20 of 2018
risk_indicators: Transaction has no apparent economic rationale
Customer provides false or inconsistent explanations
Funds trace to known illicit sources via blockchain analysis
Transaction patterns match known ML/TF typologies
Customer attempts to avoid identification or reporting
Customer conducts transactions inconsistent with their profile
Rapid movement of funds through multiple jurisdictions

title: Record Keeping and Data Retention
description: Maintain comprehensive records of all KYC/AML activities, customer information, transaction data, screening results, and regulatory filings for the required retention period. Records must be maintained in a manner that allows them to be retrieved and made available to competent authorities promptly upon request. At minimum, retain copies of all customer identification and verification documents (passport copies, utility bills, corporate documents), all CDD and EDD documentation including risk assessments, all sanctions, PEP, and adverse media screening results, all transaction monitoring alerts and investigation files, all SARs/STRs filed and supporting documentation, all CTRs and other regulatory filings, all Travel Rule data transmitted and received, all customer correspondence regarding AML/CFT matters, and all training records for AML/CFT staff. The minimum retention period varies by jurisdiction: US requires 5 years from account closure; EU/UK require 5 years from end of business relationship; Singapore requires 5 years; UAE requires 8 years. Implement data storage systems that ensure the integrity, confidentiality, and accessibility of records throughout the retention period. Ensure compliance with applicable data protection laws (GDPR in EU/UK, PDPA in Singapore) regarding the processing and storage of personal data collected for AML/CFT purposes.
threshold_triggers: All KYC/AML records from date of creation
Retention clock starts from account closure or end of business relationship
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1010.430 (5 years); EU: AMLD5 Art. 40 (5 years); UK: MLR 2017 Reg. 40 (5 years); SG: MAS Notice PSN02 Para. 13 (5 years); AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook (8 years)
risk_indicators: Records cannot be retrieved upon regulatory request
Gaps in record keeping during certain periods
Records altered or deleted before retention period expires
Failure to retain Travel Rule data
Inadequate data security resulting in data breach

title: Enhanced Due Diligence — High-Risk Customers
description: Apply enhanced due diligence measures to customers identified as high-risk through the risk assessment process. EDD goes beyond standard CDD and requires additional information gathering and verification. Obtain and verify the customer's source of wealth — the origin of the customer's total wealth and assets, not just the specific funds being used (this is broader than source of funds verification). Conduct enhanced background checks using multiple independent sources. For entity customers, obtain a detailed understanding of the entity's ownership and control structure, the nature and purpose of the business, the entity's customer base and key business relationships, and the jurisdictions in which the entity operates. Obtain senior management approval before establishing or continuing the business relationship. Implement enhanced ongoing monitoring with lower thresholds for alert generation, more frequent periodic reviews (at least annually), and more intensive scrutiny of transactions. Obtain additional documentation as warranted by the specific risk factors including certified copies of identification documents, independent verification of business activities, and references from regulated financial institutions. Document the EDD process thoroughly and maintain the file for regulatory inspection.
documents_required: Source of wealth documentation and verification
Detailed business profile and activity description
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1010.210 (risk-based program); EU: AMLD5 Art. 18; UK: MLR 2017 Reg. 33; SG: MAS Notice PSN02 Para. 10; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook Ch. 3
risk_indicators: Customer cannot provide satisfactory source of wealth evidence
Customer's wealth is disproportionate to known income sources
Customer has multiple citizenships including CBI (citizenship by investment) jurisdictions
Customer uses complex structures with no apparent tax or business rationale
Customer or UBOs have connections to high-corruption jurisdictions

title: Enhanced Due Diligence — Correspondent VASP Relationships
description: Apply enhanced due diligence to all correspondent relationships with other Virtual Asset Service Providers (VASPs) or crypto businesses. Correspondent relationships involve the provision of services by one VASP on behalf of another, including transfer facilitation, exchange services, and custody arrangements. Before entering into a correspondent relationship, gather sufficient information about the respondent VASP to fully understand the nature of its business, its regulatory status (licensed, registered, or unregulated), the jurisdiction(s) in which it operates, its AML/CFT controls, its reputation in the market, and whether it has been the subject of any regulatory enforcement action. Assess whether the respondent VASP is registered or licensed in its home jurisdiction — do not establish correspondent relationships with VASPs operating without required authorisation. Assess the adequacy of the respondent VASP's AML/CFT controls, including its CDD procedures, transaction monitoring, and SAR filing practices. Obtain senior management approval before establishing the correspondent relationship. Clearly delineate the respective AML/CFT responsibilities of each party in a written agreement. Do not permit the respondent VASP to process transactions through its accounts on behalf of the respondent VASP's own customers without appropriate controls (no payable-through accounts without full CDD).
threshold_triggers: Establishing any new correspondent VASP relationship
Annual review of existing correspondent relationships
Material change in respondent VASP's regulatory status or business model
documents_required: Respondent VASP's licence or registration certificate
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1010.210; EU: AMLD5 Art. 26; UK: MLR 2017 Reg. 34; SG: MAS Notice PSN02 Para. 10; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook Ch. 3
risk_indicators: Respondent VASP is not licensed or registered in its home jurisdiction
Respondent VASP operates in a jurisdiction with weak AML supervision
Respondent VASP has been subject to regulatory enforcement actions
Respondent VASP is unwilling to share information about its AML/CFT controls
Respondent VASP processes transactions from high-risk jurisdictions
Respondent VASP has nested or payable-through account arrangements

title: Enhanced Due Diligence — High-Risk Jurisdictions
description: Apply enhanced due diligence measures to customers, transactions, and business relationships connected to jurisdictions identified as high-risk for money laundering and terrorist financing. Maintain an up-to-date list of high-risk jurisdictions drawing from the FATF list of high-risk and other monitored jurisdictions (the grey list and black list), OFAC comprehensive sanctions programs, EU high-risk third country list (Commission Delegated Regulation), and the firm's own geographic risk assessment. For customers resident in or connected to high-risk jurisdictions, apply enhanced verification measures including obtaining additional identification documents from multiple independent sources, enhanced source of funds and source of wealth verification, more frequent periodic reviews, enhanced transaction monitoring with lower thresholds, and senior management approval for new relationships. For transactions involving high-risk jurisdictions, conduct enhanced screening and investigation. For some jurisdictions subject to comprehensive sanctions (North Korea, Iran, Cuba, Syria, Crimea region), the firm must apply a blanket prohibition on all transactions and business relationships. Maintain documented rationale for the firm's geographic risk classification methodology and update it at least semi-annually or when FATF or sanctions list updates occur.
threshold_triggers: Customer resident in FATF grey/black list jurisdiction
Business relationship with entity incorporated in high-risk jurisdiction
documents_required: Enhanced CDD documentation for affected customers
Senior management approval for high-risk jurisdiction relationships
Transaction investigation reports for flagged jurisdiction transactions
regulatory_basis: US: OFAC country sanctions programs; EU: AMLD5 Art. 18(1); UK: MLR 2017 Reg. 33(1)(b); SG: MAS Notice PSN02 Para. 10; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook
risk_indicators: Customer has nexus to FATF black-list country
Funds originate from or are destined for high-risk jurisdiction
Customer's business involves trade with sanctioned countries
Customer uses banks in jurisdictions with weak AML supervision
IP address or device data indicates connection to high-risk jurisdiction inconsistent with declared location

title: Enhanced Due Diligence — Privacy Coins and Mixing Services
description: Apply enhanced due diligence measures when customers transact in or interact with privacy-enhanced cryptocurrencies or mixing/tumbling services. Privacy coins (such as Monero/XMR, Zcash/ZEC, Dash) incorporate built-in privacy features that can obscure transaction origins, destinations, and amounts, making blockchain analysis significantly more difficult or impossible. Mixing and tumbling services (such as Tornado Cash, Wasabi Wallet CoinJoin, or other mixing protocols) obfuscate the link between the sender and recipient of crypto-assets. Establish clear policies on the firm's approach to privacy coins and mixing services — options range from full prohibition (not accepting deposits from privacy coins or wallets with mixer exposure) to risk-based acceptance with enhanced monitoring. Where the firm permits privacy coin transactions, implement enhanced monitoring including lower alerting thresholds, mandatory source of funds declarations for all privacy coin transactions regardless of value, and enhanced scrutiny of the customer's stated rationale for using privacy features. For mixer exposure detected on standard cryptocurrency deposits, require the customer to provide an explanation for the mixing activity and document the assessment. Escalate all significant privacy coin or mixer-related activity to the MLRO for review.
threshold_triggers: Blockchain analysis detects mixer/tumbler exposure on customer deposits
Customer requests to trade or withdraw privacy coins
Customer's stated purpose involves privacy-enhancing technology
documents_required: Customer explanation for mixer/privacy coin usage
regulatory_basis: US: OFAC compliance (Tornado Cash designation); EU: MiCA Art. 79 (AML requirements); UK: MLR 2017; SG: MAS Notice PSN02; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook
risk_indicators: Customer exclusively uses privacy coins without business justification
Large volume of funds passed through mixing services before deposit
Customer converts standard crypto to privacy coins immediately after purchase
Customer uses multiple mixing services in sequence (layering)
Customer's stated rationale for privacy coin usage is inconsistent with their profile

title: AML/CFT Training Programme
description: Implement and maintain a comprehensive AML/CFT training programme for all relevant staff. All employees involved in customer-facing activities, transaction processing, compliance, and management must receive AML/CFT training upon joining the firm and at least annually thereafter. Training must cover the firm's AML/CFT policies and procedures, the legal and regulatory framework applicable to the firm's activities, customer due diligence requirements, how to identify suspicious activity including jurisdiction-specific red flags, the SAR/STR filing process, sanctions obligations and the consequences of non-compliance, the Travel Rule and its implementation, record-keeping obligations, and the personal criminal liability of employees for AML/CFT failures (including tipping off). Specialised training must be provided to compliance analysts on blockchain analytics, investigation techniques, and SAR narrative writing. Senior management and board members must receive tailored training on their governance obligations, risk appetite setting, and regulatory expectations. Training must be documented including attendee lists, content covered, and assessment results. Update training content at least annually to reflect regulatory changes, new typologies, and emerging risks. Test employee knowledge through assessments and maintain records of completion.
documents_required: Regulatory change log for content updates
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1022.210(d)(3); EU: AMLD5 Art. 46; UK: MLR 2017 Reg. 24; SG: MAS Notice PSN02 Para. 14; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook Ch. 2
risk_indicators: Staff unable to identify basic suspicious activity indicators
Low SAR filing rates relative to risk exposure
Compliance failures traceable to lack of training
Staff unaware of tipping off prohibition
Training records incomplete or outdated

title: Independent AML/CFT Programme Review (Audit)
description: Conduct an independent review of the firm's AML/CFT programme at least annually to assess its adequacy and effectiveness. The review must be conducted by a qualified independent party — either an external auditor with AML/CFT expertise, an independent internal audit function (if the firm is large enough to maintain one), or a qualified external consultant. The independent reviewer must not be involved in the day-to-day operation of the AML/CFT programme. The review scope must cover the adequacy and completeness of the firm's AML/CFT policies and procedures, the effectiveness of CDD and EDD processes, the adequacy of transaction monitoring systems and rules, the quality and timeliness of SAR/STR filings, compliance with sanctions screening obligations, compliance with Travel Rule requirements, the adequacy of training programmes, the sufficiency of compliance resources and staffing, governance and oversight by senior management and the board, and compliance with record-keeping requirements. The reviewer must produce a written report identifying deficiencies, rating their severity, and recommending remediation actions with timelines. The report must be presented to the board or senior management, and management must respond with a remediation plan. Track remediation actions to completion. Retain all audit reports and remediation evidence for the required retention period.
threshold_triggers: Following significant compliance failure or enforcement action
Following material change in business model or risk profile
documents_required: Independent review scope and terms of reference
Review report with findings and recommendations
systems: External audit firms (Deloitte, EY, KPMG, PwC, specialist firms)
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1022.210(d)(4) (independent review requirement); EU: AMLD5 Art. 8; UK: MLR 2017 Reg. 21; SG: MAS Notice PSN02; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook
risk_indicators: No independent review conducted for over 12 months
Prior year's deficiencies not remediated
Reviewer lacks AML/CFT expertise or independence
Management fails to respond to findings
Review scope excludes crypto-specific risks (blockchain analytics, wallet screening)

title: Customer Offboarding — Voluntary Exit
description: Implement a structured process for customer offboarding when a customer voluntarily closes their account or terminates the business relationship. Upon receiving a closure request, verify the identity of the person making the request to ensure it is the account holder or an authorised representative. Before processing the closure, conduct a final review of the customer's account for any outstanding compliance obligations including pending transaction monitoring alerts, unresolved suspicious activity investigations, unfiled SARs/STRs, pending Travel Rule obligations, and any account holds or restrictions imposed by law enforcement or regulatory authorities. Process the return of any remaining crypto-assets or funds to the customer in accordance with the firm's withdrawal policy, ensuring that all withdrawals comply with applicable sanctions screening and Travel Rule requirements. Do not return assets if the account is subject to a freeze order, suspicious activity hold, or law enforcement request. Upon completion of offboarding, mark the account as closed in the customer management system and retain all KYC documentation, transaction records, and compliance records for the required retention period (5 years from closure in most jurisdictions, 8 years in UAE). File any pending SARs/STRs even after account closure.
threshold_triggers: Customer has been inactive for extended period and firm policy requires closure
documents_required: Final sanctions screening on withdrawal addresses
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1010.430 (record retention); EU: AMLD5 Art. 40; UK: MLR 2017 Reg. 40; SG: MAS Notice PSN02; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook
risk_indicators: Customer requests urgent closure after suspicious activity detected
Customer attempts to withdraw assets to new/unscreened wallet addresses
Large volume of transactions immediately before closure request
Account closure requested shortly after regulatory inquiry
Customer's withdrawal destination is a high-risk jurisdiction or mixer

title: Customer Offboarding — Involuntary Exit (Risk-Based Termination)
description: Implement a structured process for involuntary customer offboarding when the firm determines that the business relationship must be terminated due to AML/CFT risk concerns, inability to conduct adequate CDD, or regulatory requirements. Grounds for involuntary termination include the customer's failure to provide requested CDD or EDD documentation within a reasonable timeframe, the identification of unacceptable money laundering or terrorist financing risk that cannot be mitigated through enhanced measures, the customer's involvement in confirmed criminal activity, sanctions violations, the customer's persistent engagement in suspicious activity despite warnings, and regulatory direction to terminate the relationship. Before terminating, consult with the MLRO to assess whether termination may constitute tipping off (alerting the customer to a SAR filing or investigation). Consider whether filing a SAR before or concurrent with termination is appropriate. Provide the customer with a neutral reason for the termination that does not reference AML/CFT concerns. Process the return of remaining assets in compliance with all applicable screening and reporting requirements. In some cases, law enforcement may request that the account be kept open for monitoring purposes — accommodate such requests where legally appropriate.
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1010.210 (risk-based programme); EU: AMLD5 Art. 14(4); UK: MLR 2017 Reg. 31; SG: MAS Notice PSN02; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook
risk_indicators: Customer engages legal counsel to resist termination
Customer attempts to rapidly withdraw all assets upon notice
Customer opens multiple accounts to circumvent termination
Customer provides false information to extend the relationship
Customer threatens the firm or its staff

title: MLRO/Compliance Officer Governance and Reporting
description: Establish and maintain the role of Money Laundering Reporting Officer (MLRO) or Chief Compliance Officer with appropriate authority, independence, and resources to oversee the firm's AML/CFT programme. The MLRO must be a senior person with adequate knowledge, experience, and authority to effectively oversee the AML/CFT programme. The MLRO must have direct access to the board of directors or senior management and must report regularly on the firm's AML/CFT compliance posture. The MLRO is responsible for reviewing and approving all SAR/STR filings, overseeing the CDD/EDD and transaction monitoring functions, ensuring the firm's AML/CFT policies and procedures are current and adequate, liaising with regulators and law enforcement, and escalating material compliance issues to the board. The MLRO must produce regular compliance reports for the board including the number and nature of SARs filed, transaction monitoring alert volumes and disposition rates, KYC refresh status, sanctions screening results, training completion rates, findings from the independent review, and any regulatory correspondence or inquiries. The MLRO must be empowered to halt transactions, restrict accounts, and escalate issues without requiring prior approval from business units. Document the MLRO's appointment, qualifications, and reporting lines.
documents_required: MLRO appointment letter with defined responsibilities
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1022.210(d)(1) (compliance officer requirement); EU: AMLD5 Art. 8(4); UK: MLR 2017 Reg. 21; SG: MAS Notice PSN02; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook Ch. 2
risk_indicators: MLRO lacks sufficient authority or independence
MLRO reports to a business unit rather than the board
MLRO is overburdened with non-compliance responsibilities
Board does not receive regular compliance reports
MLRO position is vacant or filled by an unqualified person
MLRO is overridden by business units on risk decisions
